Keeping in mind 2025 is an outlier on multiple fronts because of the sale of the casino segment and the special dividend payment, the underlying point here is that, notwithstanding one-time payments made to Italy for the aforementioned contract, operating cash flow has tended to be a hair above $1 billion annually. CapEx eats some of that, but even the increased regular dividend at 23¢ per quarter only amounts to about $172 million annually, which is easily affordable here. At these prices, finding a stock with that high of a yield and one that's so easily covered by its operations makes Brightstar particularly appealing. But what about the rest of the cash generated above and beyond the dividend?

That's where share repurchases come in. Last year, Brightstar bought back around 16.2 million, spending around half of the authorized $500 million repurchase. More buybacks are to be expected, and in February’s conference call, they reported another 2.1 million were bought back in early 2026. Since the stock price has fallen in recent months, Brightstar should be getting more bang for their buck with this year’s portion of the buyback. Eliminating shares through the buyback only makes the dividend more sustainable going forward, and in my view, raises the possibility of increasing it further in the future. That's particularly true if the company's strategy gets them back to meaningful revenue growth.

A Lot Depends on Italy

Perhaps it goes without saying, but investing €2.2 billion both creates an asset and necessarily a risk. Brightstar can no longer afford to view Italy as a side business. This investment means Italy absolutely needs to remain a winner for them and needs to continue growing and generating money for them. If Italy doesn’t grow enough to justify their big expense, Brightstar’s management could look very foolish for bidding so hard on the Italy Lotto concession.

The company is similarly trying to grow its business online through what it calls iLottery. While this remains less significant than the physical business in the United States and Italy, it could be another cash sink for the company in that we don’t really know it will ultimately generate revenue commensurate with the investment being made in it. The potential is there, so be sure, but in breaking new ground in the business, they will also run into unforeseeable new challenges.

Brightstar wanted to be a pure-play lottery company, and that means any challenges related to their lottery business will reflect more directly on the company. Not diversifying across multiple markets confers risk, even if the lottery is seemingly a relatively stable generator of cash.

The Price is Right, the Dividend is Better

While the company's restructuring as a pure-play lottery provider is a work in progress, I’m rating Brightstar as a buy, especially after the stock fell to new 52-week lows in recent weeks. The earnings valuation is in line with expectations, but the high and readily supportable dividend yield really makes this stock shine at these prices, particularly in an uncertain market. As mentioned before, lotteries are historically resilient even in difficult economic times, which we may or may not be heading into.

Investors should keep a close eye on Italy Lotto, because clearly as that goes, so goes Brightstar. So long as the cash flow is enough to sustain the dividend, though, which right now it plainly is, the stock seems substantially undervalued.

I think Brightstar Lottery is ultimately an unconventional flight to safety stock, and its yield makes it a promising contrarian play at a time when opportunities for safety are few and far between.

data.title: Polaris: Not Attractive Enough Yet
data.description:
The savvy and valuation-conscious investor with a fair bit of luck could make a case that my thesis on Polaris, given in the article found here, was ill-timed and that profit could have been made by investing in Polaris (PII) contrary to my forecast. While this would be true, the return that I believe most investors are likely to have as a result of the company is, for the time being, negative, as per the following RoR and underperformance.

Polaris is a trickier business than investors give it credit for. Many analysts cite Polaris as being a safe and reliable manufacturer of off-road vehicles, even with military and other appealing applications. They, however, miss that the company, in the past few years, has been tested on exactly these assumptions, and in many cases failed to live up to its expectations - and the proof of this is both in results and other analyzable factors in the companies.

Whenever something is coined as being a "value investment", I tend to pay attention. Many times, what I am seeing is, in fact, a value investment, an attractive one, that I can invest in. Other times, however, what we turn out to have is an investment that, for one reason or another, is in fact not a value investment at all, but a mix of a value trap and a company that has seen its "best days" come and go.

The two sides for the company we're looking at are basically one saying that this is a value investment, with recent results reinforcing the value proposition, and the other, to which I belong, saying that this is not necessarily a value investment and needs to be priced contrary to how it used to be viewed a few years ago.

In this article, I will look at my thesis given 4 months ago, what signs there are that I should upgrade my $40 PT that I gave for the company in that piece, and what (if any) signs there are that things are not looking up at all.

Polaris - Upside is There if You Premiumize or if It Turns Around

The idea that Polaris is an investment that has trickier upside and fundamentals than investors seem willing to give it is hardly a new one, nor one that's underrepresented by the market today. Both Wall Street and Quant have the company at a conservative "HOLD" rating, with the SA average being "BUY" currently, but there are also indicators in other articles and ratings that things are not, in fact, looking up.

The often-cited issue is growth - as in there is very little or none at all. In addressing the fiscal 2025 period, we find that Polaris reported sales of about $7B+. Far from being a growth number, this was actually flat compared to previous year results.

Why is this such a problem?

Because we're in a high inflation environment, by most metrics. In a high inflation environment, a flat revenue number does not actually imply "flat", it implies a decline. In an inflationary environment, I would argue that Polaris needed to grow 3-6% just to keep pace. It did not. Growth stalls in high inflation mean (or can mean) that the end market for the company's products, in this case recreational vehicles (specialized) and specialized automotives, is difficult.

When I build a more conservative, or bearish-considered, thesis, I look for signs that are objectively valid for that being the case. On a high level, many of the company's volumes saw a decline. RV sales are down 10% in 2025, for instance. But there are also signs that Polaris is struggling to increase its sales, despite what can only be described as significant promotions. In 2025, the company's gross margins were under pressure by what analysts reported as being higher promotional activity in order for dealers to move inventory. This is never a good sign. Despite shipment volumes being up, the net pricing (meaning what money the company gets to keep per sold vehicle) is down for many quarters now.

This tells me that Polaris is actually prioritizing not profitable sales but sales volumes. When a company is prioritizing volumes over profit, that's dangerous - and I see no indications in the recent results that this is actually changing in a meaningful way - the other way around, actually.

Reduction in dealer inventory levels is now down, but this is a result of the pressure mentioned above - powersports, which is the industry that Polaris is in, continues to be down.

Furthermore, in 2026, when Polaris reported its numbers, management cited very conservative estimates. Rather than expecting significant growth in the top line, the company now expects only 1-3% for the year. That means that 2026 is not going to be in any way a "turnaround" year for Polaris.

The structural decline in demand for the company's products can also be viewed in the context of asset closures. Polaris has quite recently closed its Wisconsin plant, and this speaks in favor of a leaner, more realistic Polaris - which is a good thing—but it works against the assumption that this is only a "transition" in the industry and more in favor of a new reality.

A good exercise is asking what would be needed for the industry to see a complete turnaround. As in, a full recovery to where the company used to be.

This is just my view, of course, but in order for that to materialize, I would need to see lower interest rates. I consider that unlikely. Lower interest rates would be important because they would motivate consumers to spend money on what I consider to be high-priced discretionary items, like this. But again, both macro and consumer credit standards (tighter than before) actually speak against this happening.

Polaris specifically also needs to reduce its reliance on external materials and inputs. The company is already doing this by planning to reduce China exposure, but the question is whether it's enough.

So, Polaris is, from some perspectives, doing what it can to address some of these issues. However, for Polaris to really get past and move into a new reality, I believe the company needs to take another step forward. I believe Polaris needs to move from a "push" to a "pull" model. What this means is that part of the reason for the volatility was the ZIRP and pandemic boom, which clouded inventory levels and demands. It needs to move to a model where it only produces what is already selling or is sold. This means a few things. It likely means that volatility is going to be less than before, but lower valuations are due to more realistic, but lower, sales numbers. But it also enables the company to move away from strategies that require it to promote old stock at margin-destroying levels, which not only hurts the bottom line but, more importantly, destroys the premium image of the brand. It's the same thing that I argue happened with BMW (BMWKY) and Mercedes when they introduced substantial financing options and lower-end entry cars. It may have increased sales, and I'm favorable at least on BMW, but it also killed at least a part of the view that the brands are "premium, above others".

A brand like Polaris needs to be very careful not only in what it does but also in how what it does impacts its brand. This is not helped by the quality issues that have now been a part of its story for several years.

To justify a higher valuation, Polaris needs to do, in my view, many of these things (some the company is already doing), and then I'd be willing to once again consider it a "BUY" at a more "reasonable" level. Remember, I used to own stock in the business, but I got out between 2021 and 2022.

What does the current valuation tell us?

Valuation for Polaris - Upside Requires More Clarity; I'm Not Moving My PT

Not much that is interesting to me, unfortunately. The current analyst estimates are inflated insofar as they mostly seem to be in favor of a complete reversal of the company, with a 127% annualized EPS growth rate based on 17-18 analysts.

While I concur that "a" recovery will happen, I remain very skeptical as to the exact nature and level of this recovery and what this will mean for the valuation. The expectations above are based on a 90-100x P/E, which I consider to be completely speculative and unrealistic.

If we estimate the company at a 20-year average of about 16x P/E, we arrive at a price of around $65/share, similar to the targets I cited in my last article, found here.

The problem is that right now the company trades at $55/share, and despite an AEPS that doesn't cover the dividend for 2025, it is unlikely to cover it for 2026, and the upside, even with the close to 5% yield toward that target, is less than 13% per year.

So even in the case of a normalization to a 20-year average, to an AEPS that rises to more than 200% of the currently expected 2026E $1.65/share by 2028E, even under those assumptions, Polaris is not expected to outperform my hurdle rate of 15% per year. Furthermore, the company's historical accuracy does not speak in favor of these levels even occurring, and based on the risks I cited above, I would consider them even less realistic than that.

My estimate of about $40/share in PT may be considered excessively low, but it's also a PT that actually shows, if it drops that far, a considerable and, above all, realistic upside to a more conservative recovery. Given all of the challenges that the company faces, I would avoid being blinded by what I see as a company facing a new reality (not a transition), and therefore I would consider Polaris to be a "HOLD" here, and my $40/share PT to be unchanged and still valid after 4 months' time.

Risks & Considerations

I continue to believe in 2026 that PII has more risks than most shareholders seem to be currently willing to give the company. The series of failures and recalls has, I believe, dented the customer's faith in the company, and this remains a headwind. While the company has a very solid market position, Ducati, BRP (DOO), CFMOTO, and Kubota (KUBTY) have alternatives for many of the company's segments, and some of those are also valued more attractively.

All in all, even if the company were to trade at historical multiples and we had the 2027E EPS in this year, I still believe the resulting P/E would be too high for the company in its current state, and I see no reason for me to change my assumptions on this one.

Thesis

Polaris was once a company I invested in and thought well of. Following tariff and macro impacts, as well as a series of recalls, and mechanical and quality issues with the company's products, it's unclear to me where the company is going from here, and the picture remains clouded in 2026. While it's technically a qualitative business (provided it manages to turn things around, at least), I would be careful about investing here, and I would be even more careful about investing at a high valuation.

A low price is a must for me for this company. I believe anything above $40/share PT to be illusory for the longer term. Thus, I consider the company to be a "HOLD" here and not worthy of a conservative investment. This makes me one of the analysts with the lowest PTs, but given the trends, that's something I'm very comfortable with. The dividend is not well covered now or likely for 2026, based on current assumptions.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 2/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a price target of $40/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.
